_meta.description: Plain-language definitions for WCAG and compliance terms used across the dashboard. Surfaced inline via the <dfn data-glossary='key'> markup + the GlossaryController in app.js. Authored 2026-05-22; needs ongoing compliance-team review.

wcag.label: WCAG
wcag.definition: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — the international standard that defines how to make web content usable by people with disabilities. The current version is WCAG 2.2.

aa.label: AA
aa.definition: WCAG conformance level AA. The legal floor in most jurisdictions; covers contrast, keyboard access, captioning, and resizable text.

aaa.label: AAA
aaa.definition: The highest WCAG conformance level. Requires 7:1 text contrast, sign-language interpretation for video, and other strict criteria. Required for shareable Luqen reports and the verdict badge.

blocking-issue.label: blocking issue
blocking-issue.definition: An accessibility error that prevents people with disabilities from using a critical part of the site. Counted separately from warnings and notices.

mandatory-failure.label: mandatory failure
mandatory-failure.definition: An accessibility issue that violates a named regulatory rule in a jurisdiction the site has been checked against. Distinct from raw WCAG errors that aren't tied to a regulation.

jurisdiction.label: jurisdiction
jurisdiction.definition: A legal area whose accessibility regulations apply to a site (for example, EU under EAA, US under ADA Title III). Each jurisdiction maps to one or more named regulations and a subset of WCAG rules.

regulation.label: regulation
regulation.definition: A specific accessibility law or technical standard, such as EN 301 549 (EU), Section 508 (US federal), or BITV 2.0 (Germany). One regulation usually covers a defined set of WCAG criteria.

selector.label: selector
selector.definition: A CSS path that identifies the exact element on the page where an issue was found, e.g. main > article:nth-child(2) > img.banner. Used to locate and fix the issue in the source code.

alt-text.label: alt text
alt-text.definition: Short written description of an image, read by screen readers and used when the image fails to load. Required by WCAG 1.1.1 for all informative images.

contrast-ratio.label: contrast ratio
contrast-ratio.definition: The relative brightness difference between text and its background, expressed as a ratio from 1:1 (no contrast) to 21:1 (pure black on pure white). WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large; AAA requires 7:1 and 4.5:1.

aria.label: ARIA
aria.definition: Accessible Rich Internet Applications — HTML attributes (role, aria-label, aria-describedby, etc.) that describe a widget's purpose and state to assistive technology. Misused ARIA is often worse than no ARIA.

screen-reader.label: screen reader
screen-reader.definition: Software that reads web content aloud or outputs it to a braille display. Common screen readers: NVDA and JAWS on Windows, VoiceOver on Apple platforms, TalkBack on Android.

keyboard-navigation.label: keyboard navigation
keyboard-navigation.definition: Operating the site without a mouse, using only Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, arrow keys, and Escape. WCAG 2.1.1 requires every interactive element to be reachable and operable from the keyboard.

focus-ring.label: focus ring
focus-ring.definition: The visible outline that appears around an element when it receives keyboard focus. Required by WCAG 2.4.7; must be removed only when replaced with an equally visible indicator.

landmark.label: landmark
landmark.definition: A semantic region of the page identified by an HTML element (header, nav, main, aside, footer) or an ARIA role. Screen-reader users navigate by landmarks; missing or duplicated landmarks make the page harder to scan.

eaa.label: EAA
eaa.definition: European Accessibility Act — EU directive 2019/882, effective 2025-06-28. Mandates accessibility for digital products and services sold in the EU; references the technical standard EN 301 549.

ada.label: ADA
ada.definition: Americans with Disabilities Act — US federal civil-rights law. Title II's 2024 DOJ final rule requires state and local government websites to meet WCAG 2.1 AA. Title III governs private 'places of public accommodation' and is enforced via case law, where courts regularly cite WCAG as the conformance benchmark even though no DOJ regulation mandates it for private sites.

psbar.label: PSBAR
psbar.definition: Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018 — UK regulations requiring public-sector websites and mobile apps to meet the EN 301 549 standard (which cites WCAG 2.1 AA), publish an accessibility statement, and provide a feedback mechanism for complaints.

en-301-549.label: EN 301 549
en-301-549.definition: European harmonised standard for accessibility requirements of ICT products and services. Cited by the EAA as the technical means of complying with the directive.

verdict.label: verdict
verdict.definition: Luqen's one-sentence summary of a scan's compliance status. Maps jurisdictional results to four states: compliant (no mandatory failures, no errors), warn (mandatory pass but raw errors remain), needs review (manual review required), non-compliant (mandatory failures).

fix-proposal.label: fix proposal
fix-proposal.definition: An AI-generated suggestion for how to remediate a specific accessibility issue. Includes the rationale (why it's broken), the selector (where), and a code snippet (what to change). Always verify before applying.
